text.title: NoSQL Anti-patterns
text.summary: Forcing relational patterns, unbounded arrays, missing TTL, hot partitions, and schemaless rot.

antipattern.description: Choosing MongoDB "because microservices" without documenting access patterns first.
antipattern.reason: Postgres JSONB covers 80% of flexible-schema use cases; premature NoSQL adoption locks into a model that may not match future query needs.

antipattern.description: Embedding arrays with unbounded growth in MongoDB (e.g., appending every event to a user document).
antipattern.reason: Documents grow past the 16MB limit; large documents degrade working set, increase index size, and slow full-document updates.

antipattern.description: Missing indexes on MongoDB fields used in query predicates.
antipattern.reason: Unindexed queries cause collection scans; at scale this degrades from milliseconds to minutes.

antipattern.description: Choosing a Cassandra/DynamoDB partition key without modeling the primary access pattern.
antipattern.reason: Partition key is immutable at scale; a wrong choice requires a full table copy to fix, causing downtime.

antipattern.description: Storing Redis keys without TTL or eviction policy.
antipattern.reason: Keys accumulate until maxmemory is hit; Redis then either evicts randomly or rejects writes, causing application errors.

antipattern.description: Agent uses raw keys in Redis without namespace prefix (user:123 vs {service}:user:123).
antipattern.reason: Multiple services sharing one Redis instance collide on identical key names, causing cross-service data corruption.

rule.statement: Document access patterns before choosing a store; start with Postgres until a specific access pattern (time-series, graph, cache) explicitly justifies a NoSQL store.
rule.rationale: Premature NoSQL adoption trades ACID guarantees and ad-hoc query flexibility for operational complexity that rarely pays off at small scale.

text.reference: Heuristic script mapping access-pattern keywords to recommended store
